LAW AND MOTION, 302, PETITIONERS ANDREW HOYEM AND DIANA KETCHAM'S MOTION TO CONFIRM ARBITRATION DECISION AND AWARD SUBJECT TO AMENDMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, CORRECTION. NO APPEARANCES, TENTATIVE RULING ADOPTED AS FOLLOWS: PETITIONERS' PETITION TO CONFIRM ARBITRATION AWARD IS DENIED WITHOUT PREJUDICE. THE COURT REMANDS THIS MATTER BACK TO THE ARBITRATOR TO CONSIDER THE RATE OF PREJUDGMENT INTEREST AND THE DATE UPON WHICH PREJUDGMENT INTEREST SHOULD COMMENCE. THE ARBITRATOR RETAINS JURISDICTION TO ADJUDICATE THE PREJUDGMENT INTEREST ISSUE. PETITIONERS ARE ORDERED TO CONTACT THE ARBITRATOR WITHIN 30 DAYS TO SCHEDULE A HEARING REGARDING THE PREJUDGMENT INTEREST ISSUES. ORDER SIGNED. (SEE MINI-MINUTES OR ORDER SIGNED THIS DATE FOR THE COURT'S COMPLETE RULING) JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: JACQUELINE ALAMEDA; NOT REPORTED. (302/EPS)

MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PETITIONERS ANDREW HOYEM AND DIANA KETCHAM'S MOTION TO CONFIRM ARBITRATION AWARD.

Motion granted. No opposition filed.

Order signed. Separate judgment signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/EPS)

Petitioners Notice Of Motion And Motion To Confirm Arbitration Decision And Award Subject To Amendment Or, In The Alternative, Correction; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Apr-07-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
PETITIONERS ANDREW HOYEM and DIANA KETCHAM'S Motion To Confirm Arbitration Decision And Award Subject To Amendment Or, In The Alternative, Correction.

No appearances. The tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Petitioners' petition to confirm arbitration award is denied without prejudice. The Court remands this matter back to the arbitrator to consider the rate of prejudgment interest and the date upon which prejudgment interest should commence. (See DiMarco v. Chaney (1995) 31 Cal.App.4th 1809, 1816-1818 [remanding matter back to arbitrator to determine amount of fees and costs]; Mossman v. City of Oakdale (2009) 170 Cal.App.4th 83, 93-94 [remanding matter back to arbitrator to determine what relief would make petitioner whole].) "There is nothing to prevent this court from ordering that the matter be resubmitted to the arbitrator for further clarification of the award." (Mossman, 170 Cal.App.4th at 92.)

The record demonstrates that the prejudgment issues were never litigated and the arbitrator merely carried forward numbers that were set forth in the parties' settlement agreement. (Sepuya Decl., Ex. 3.) Petitioners contend that these numbers were the result of mutual or unilateral mistake. The arbitrator should resolve this issue on the merits.

The arbitrator retains jurisdiction to adjudicate the prejudgment interest issue.

"California's contractual arbitration law permits arbitrators to issue an amended award to resolve an issue omitted from the original award through the mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect of the arbitrator if the amendment is made before judicial confirmation of the original award, is not inconsistent with other findings on the merits of the controversy, and does not cause demonstrable prejudice to the legitimate interests of any party." (A.M. Classic Const., Inc. v. Tri-Build Development Co. (1999) 70 Cal.App.4th 1470, 1478; see also Delaney v. Dahl (2002) 99 Cal.App.4th 647.) Those circumstances are present here.

Petitioners are ordered to contact the arbitrator within 30 days to schedule a hearing regarding the prejudgment interest issues.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Not Reported. (302/EPS)
